text: CAROLINES, or CAROLINAS, a general denomination sometimes given to all the islands in the South seas, lying between the parallels of 5° and 10° N lat., and extending E to the Philippine islands. These islands—sometimes also called Palaos, and sometimes New Philippines—are said to be in number about thirty, but are little known to Europeans. The largest, Hogolu, situated in the E extremity of the group, is in length about 90 m., and in breadth about 40 m. Yap, in the W extremity, is the next in size, and is about two-thirds less: the others are very small. The inhabitants are Malays, and are said to resemble those of the Philippines. They were named Carolinas from an island which Lazeano discovered in 1686, and named in honour of his sovereign Carlos II. of Spain.—Also a small island in the S Pacific, in the Low archipelago, in S lat. 9° 30′, and W long. 150° 30′.
CAROLUS POORT, a pass of South Africa, in the district and 90 m. NNE of Graaff-Reynet.

CAROMBE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. and 6 m. NE of Carpentras. Pop. in 1841, 2,573. It contains a fortress, and has an active commerce in wine, olive-oil, and legumes. The Florence iris is extensively cultivated in the environs.
CARONA, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, 3 m. SSW of Lugano, near the r. bank of the lake of that name. Pop. (Catholic) 514.
CARONDELET, formerly called VIDE-POCHE, a village of St. Louis co., in the state of Missouri, on the W bank of the Mississippi, 5 m. below St. Louis. Pop. in 1840, 2,093. It was founded in 1764.
CARON-UWCH-CLAWDD, or STRATA FLORIDA, a chapelry in the p. of Caron-ys-Clawdd, Cardiganshire. Pop. in 1841, 819.
CARON-YS-CLAWDD, a parish of Cardiganshire, 204 m. WNW of London, on the Berwyn, and near the confluence of the Teife. Pop. in 1841, 2,572.

CARONI, or CARONY, a river of Venezuela, which is supposed to issue from the Sierra Pacaraina, on the confines of Brazil, runs N, receiving on the r. the Acanum, and on the l. the Paragua, and after a total course of 300 m., falls into the Orinoco, 20 m. N of a town of the same name, and 75 m. W of Angostura. Its waters are remarkable for their purity; and the impetuosity with which they are carried into the Orinoco, renders them distinwaters of that river. The rapidity of its current, | the rocky character of its channel, and a fall of 15 ft. which occurs near its mouth, render the C. wholly unnavigable. - Also a river of Trinidad, flowing into the gulf of Paria. It is navigable about 1 m. above Saint Joseph for canoes and bateaux.

CARONIA, a town of Sicily, in the prov. of Messina, district and 6 m. NE of Mistretta, near the entrance of a small river of the same name into the Mediterranean. Pop. 1,800. It possesses a small port and a custom-house; and has a considerable commerce in corn and oil.

CARORA, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. and 100 m. SSW of Coro, on a river of the same name, an affluent of the Tocuya, and 50 m. from Lake Maracaybo. This town, which before the war of independence was extremely flourishing, with a pop. of about 9,000, is generally well-built; the streets are broad and well laid out; and it contains a handsome church and a chapel. It formerly possessed extensive manufactories of saddlery, shoes, &c. The rearing of cattle, and the manufacture of leather and of hammocks of the fibres of the Agave fætida, now form the chief occupations of the inhabitants. The surrounding savannah district, although extremely warm and liable to drought, is salubrious, and is celebrated for its aromatic herbs, and a species of cochineal.
CAROTTE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 25 m. SE of Trent, on the Astico.

CAROTTO, a village of Naples, in the prov. and 18 m. SE of Naples, district and 6 m. SW of Castela-Mare, near the Mediterranean. Pop. 3,500. It contains 2 churches, and a school-of-navigation founded by Frederick IV. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.

CAROUGE, or Carogio, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, on the l. bank of the Arve, which is crossed a little way below by a handsome stone bridge, and less than a mile S of Geneva. Pop. 4,367. The houses are generally only one story in height, and the streets are broad and straight. It contains a church, a synagogue, a college, and an hospital; and has manufactories of fine leather, china, chronometers, a cotton spinning-mill, and a tile-work, and possesses an active commerce in wine and brandy. Fairs are held twice a-year, and markets twice a-year.—This town, originally a mere village, was in 1780 constituted by the king of Sardinia cap. of a new province with important privileges annexed. In 1816 the district and town were ceded to Switzerland.

CAROUGE, formerly an administrative prov. of Savoy, comprising an area of 60,590 hectares; bounded on the N by Switzerland; on the E and S by the provinces of Faucigny and Genevois; and on the W by the Rhone; and containing in 1821, 42,991 inhabitants. It comprised 4 mandementos, viz., Annemasse, Saint-Julien, Reignier, and Seyssel, and 71 communes. The cap. was Carouge. In 1837 it was partitioned amongst the adjacent provinces.—Also a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district and 4 m. WNW of Oron, and 8 m. NNE of Lausanne, at an alt. of 2,470 ft. above sea-level. Pop. (Protestant) 431.
CAROUGES, a town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, 17 m. NW of Alencon. Pop. 1,950. It contains an iron-work and tanneries, and has an annual fair. In the environs are some iron-mines.
CAROVERES, an aboriginal tribe of SE Africa, who inhabit a district to the W of Mozambique, near Lake Maravi.
CAROVIGNO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Otranto, district and 21 m. WNW of Brindisi, on a hill. Pop. 3,576. It contains an hospital and two convents. CAR
CAROVILLE, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Sannio or Molise, district and 10 m. NNE of Isernia, and 20 m. WNW of Campo-basso. Pop. 2,019.

CARP (LAKE), a lake of North British America, in the district of the Coppermine Indians, one of the chain of lakes which unites by Yellow-knife river with the Great Slave lake.-Also a lake of New Caledonia, between the ranges of the Peak and Babine mountains, in N lat. 56° 45', which forms one of the principal sources of the SW branch of the Liards or Mountain river.—Also a lake in the same district, to the SW of M'Leod's lake, in N lat. 54° 45'.—Also a river in the state of Michigan, U. S., which flows into a lake of that name, opposite the Manitou islands.

CARPANE, or CARPANEDO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, prov. and 23 m. NNE of Vicenza, and 9 m. NNW of Bassano, on the l. bank of the Brenta. Pop. 1,900. It has some iron-works.
CARPANETO, a village of Parma, in the district and 13 m. WNW of Borgo-San-Donino.
CARPAS, a town in the island of Cyprus, near its NE extremity, 65 m. NE of Nicosia, near the site of the ancient Carpasia. It has a small port, from which timber forms the chief article of export.

CARPATHIANS, or KRAPACKS, a mountainsystem of Europe, chiefly belonging to Hungary. A great mountainous belt commences at the outlet of the Elbe into Saxony; runs along the NE of Bohemia and Moravia; forms the S border of Austrian Silesia, whence a lateral ridge strikes off to the S, dividing Moravia from Upper Hungary; and then, running almost due E, separates Austrian Poland from the great valley watered by the Danube and its numerous tributary streams. It is at the point where the lateral ridge commences, while the grand chain continues its E direction, that it obtains the German appellation of Crapack,—probably its original name softened by the Roman enunciation. This enormous ridge has been celebrated from remote antiquity; but, unlike the Alps of Italy and Switzerland, it has not yet been minutely explored by the eye of science; and no skilful orologist, like Saussure, has yet ascended its summits, traversed its valleys, explored its recesses, or examined its strata. Hence it is, that though the general direction of the chain is known, no accurate description of its different bearings or occasional changes of direction, its lateral ridges, or its principal summits with their respective altitudes, has yet been given to the public. The lofty rock on which the castle of Presburg is situated, is generally regarded as forming the W extremity of the C. From Presburg, the range extends for 100 m. in a direction a little to the E of N, presenting summits about 2,000 ft. in height. This portion of the chain is known as the Little C., or the White mountains. In about the lat. of 50° the range turns E, and runs about 200 m. in that direction, under the successive appellations of the Magura, the Babagura, and the Beykid. From the Babagura mountains the isolated mass of the Tatra is separated by the valleys of the Areva and the Donajec; from the Beykid, by the river Poprad; and from the Erzgebirge, or Ore mountains, by the upper course of the Waag. Of the Krivan and the Lomnitz peaks in the Tatra, Mr. Paget says that the former "rises so immediately from the plain, with its conical form and fine rocky summit, and towers so gloriously above all its neighbours, that it gave him a finer idea of a vast mountain than any other he had before seen." The Lomnitz is equally distinct in outline, and presents a still more impressive scene of stern and rugged sub- The main ridge of the C., extending E limity. 现在那个人们也有有有的那种的现在分词 网络阿尔斯尼斯尼斯斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯尼斯 from the Tatra, is known as the Waldgebirge or Forest mountains. This portion of the range is about 200 m. in length, with a breadth varying from 30 to 50 m. Two great roads pass over the Waldgebirge into Russia. The highest points of the chain belong to Hungary, and occur in the ridge called Tatra. The following are the most remarkable peaks belonging to it, as measured by Wahlenberg: Feet. Great Lomnitzer Spitzi, in N lat. 49° 2', E long. 20° 16', On the S side of the Waag is a smaller range—the Erzgebirge, or Ore mountains of Hungary—running parallel to the Great Tatra, the highest of which is Dumbier, 6,576 ft. above the level of the sea. The central and highest parts of these mountains consist of granite, composed of quartz and milk-white feldspar, with very little mica. At a lower level greywacke and transition limestone appear. The latter is most abundant on the N side of the mountain, but seems to be nearly wanting on the S side. Kralova Hola, at the source of the Waag, has an alt. of 5,700 ft. The Carpathians to the NE of the Tatra, in the counties of Urgh, Beregh, and Marmarosh, according to Kitaibel, who examined them, are equally lofty with the Tatra, those especially on the frontiers of Galicia and the Buckowine; but they are not so steep as the Tatra, nor covered with so many huge and bare rocks; they also rest on a broader base, and their summits are not so sharp nor pointed.
