Fichsee, 4,807 Kukuratzo, 4,988 Schemnitz (mining town of), 2,172 Gyongos (Upper Hungary), 620 Tokay (Do)., 470 Semlin, 290 The Carpathians to the E and S of Transylvania have not yet been explored, nor their elevations taken. Near the sources of the Alauta, the eastern C. ridge divides into two branches. That branch which runs along the l. bank of the Alauta, forms the boundaries betwixt Transylvania and the Turkish principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia. The principal summits which rise here are the Nagy-Hagymas, the Kaszony, the Piatra-Laptuie, the Tatara, and the Magura. From Pojana-Mujeri to their termination at the Danube, the C. run W by SW through Transylvania and the Banat, and along the Turkish frontiers. The Mali-Stirbacz, belonging to this section of the chain, which reaches the Danube and causes that river to form a cataract, seems to be the connecting link betwixt the Balkan and Carpathian systems. Passes.] There are a number of passes in the C. system, which may be easily defended against invading armies. The pass of Jablunkawer leads S from Austrian Silesia into Upper Hungary. - Another noted pass is at the source of the Theiss, and leads into Pocutia and the Buckowine.—The third pass is at the source of the Bistriza, and leads into the to the SE of Hermanstadt, described by Dr. Clarke as presenting truly Alpine scenery; and where the rapid Alauta, after many turnings and windings among tremendous snowy cliffs narrowing its channel, at last escapes through rugged defiles, and enters the Wallachian plains. This pass was the scene of one of Bem's most brilliant exploits in the late revolutionary war of Hungary.—The ninth pass leads through the Irongate branch into the Banat of Temeswar, and is called the grotto of Veterani. These passes were all well-fortified, to prevent the entrance of the Turks into Transylvania and the Banat; but several of them were nevertheless forced by the Turks, who blew up the grotto of Veterani in the campaign of 1788.—Another pass named the Deva, leads from Transylvania to the Banat, on the S .- The last pass worthy of notice is in the W chain leading into Upper Hungary, by way of the river Keres or Creus, which must be crossed no less than thirty times, during its passage through the chain, before the traveller can enter the Hungarian plains. The whole extent of the Crapack mountains, beginning at Presburg, and running thence NE, then SE, and afterwards SW, till we arrive at the Banat of Temeswar, cannot be less than 900 m., including the circuit that encloses Transylvania. Vegetation.] From the level of the Danube to the summit of the C., Hungary presents five zones or belts, rising successively. 1. The plains rich in corn and fruit trees, extending to the first hills, or nearly 1,500 ft. above the sea. 2. The woody region, or the region where the oak, beech, and chestnut thrive. The termination of the beech is at the height of 4,194 ft. above the sea. This region is richer in plants than the same region in the Alps of Switzerland. 3. The Sub-Alpine region commences where the beech terminates, and where the Pinus abies or Scotch fir begins, and exhibits nearly the same plants as in Switzerland; but as we ascend this region, a striking difference takes place. The gloomy and useless moss-pine begins to cover the ground at 4,476 ft. above the sea, and the Scotch fir terminates at an elevation of 4,902 ft.; whereas in the same region in Switzerland, the Scotch fir vegetates at the height of 5,862 ft. above the sea, or 960 ft. higher than on the C. mountains. 4. The lower Alpine region, or that of the moss-pine, extends from the termination of the Scotch fir, to where the moss-plant rises only to the height of 2 ft. in open places. This region abounds in moss-pines, under whose shade large and fine plants grow and thrive at a greater elevation than do the same in the Alps of Switzerland; where the moss-pines do not appear, the earth is barren, and produces a few mountain blue-bells and other small flowers. The upper boundary of this region is 5,968 ft. above the sea; but in particular places among the stones, the muglius is seen penetrating the soil at an elevation of - 6,395 ft. above the sea. 5. The superior Alpine region commences where the muglius terminates, at 5,968 ft., and continues to the highest peak of the C. All large vegetables disappear at this elevation. This region has a most barren and dreary aspect, being in great part covered with naked stones, and the rocks with dark lichens. A few plants, as the dwarf primrose, mountain blue-bell, gentian, and dwarf saw-worth, and other diminutive plants, may be seen here and there, and the last was found in great abundance at Kalbachergrat by Wahlenberg, being an elevation of 7,141 ft. above the sea. It is surprising that none of the C. are covered with perpetual snow, not even the peak of Lomnitz, though in the latitude of 49°. Wahlenberg endeavours to account for this anomaly, by the prevalence of the hot winds from the plains of Hungary, which are the most extensive in the S of Europe, and where the summer-heats are very great.

CARPENENDOLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gulf of Milan, in the prov. and 15 m. SE of Brescia, 4 m. SSE of Montechiari, near the l. bank of the Chiesa. Pop. 4,500. It contains 2 churches, an orphan - asylum, an hospital, and an old castle. A fair is held once a-year.
CARPENETTO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 8 m. E of Acqui. Pop. 1,600.

CARPENTARIA (GULF OF), an immense gulf on the N coast of Australia, between the parallels of 13° and 18° S, and the meridians of 136° and 142° E. It received its name from James Carpentier, a Dutch captain, who first explored it in 1627. Capt. Flinders in 1803 entered it, from Torres straits, and fixed the position of its principal points, down to the Wellesley islands at the bottom of the gulf. Capt. Stokes explored its S shore for an extent of 200 m. in 1839-40. Commencing at Cape York, on the NE extremity of the coast, we have a low unbroken coast-line running to the head of the gulf, and presenting only two or three low capes or points throughout its whole extent of 500 m. Among these points are Duythen point and Cape Turnagain. "The general appearance of the head of the gulf is that of a low mangrove shore, between 10 and 30 ft. high, over which the interior is not visible from the offing." [Stokes.] Three large rivers flow into the head of the gulf, viz., the Van Diemen, Flinders, and Albert. From a point mid-way between the two latter rivers, the coast trends NW to the parallel of 15°, where, at the head of Limmen bight, it turns NNE. This portion of the coast presents several groups of which the principal are the Wellesley isles and the Pellew isles. From Limmen bight, the outline of the coast becomes very irregular. The principal indentations in this quarter are Blue-mud bay, Caledon bay, Melville bay, and Arnhem bay; the principal capes, Cape Barrow, Cape Shield, Point Arrowsmith, Point Alexander, Cape Arnhem, and Cape Wilberforce; and the principal islands, Groote island, Bickerton island, Woodah island, Melville island, Inglis island, Cunningham island, and the Wessel islands. The middle of the upper part of the gulf has not yet been explored.

CARPENTER'S LANDING, a village of Greenwich township, Gloucester co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., on Mantua creek, 7 m. above its entrance into the Delaware, and 40 m. SW of Trenton. Pop. in 1840, about 250.

CARPENTRAS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse. —The arrond. comprises an area of 84,189 hectares, and 4 cantons, viz., Carpentras, Mormoiron, Pernes, and Sault. Pop. in 1831, 51,269; in 1836, 52,699; and in 1846, 55,714. — The cant. contains 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 27,339; in 1840, 28,577.—The town is CAR situated on the l. bank of the Auzon, at the foot of Mont-Ventoux, at an alt. of 318 ft. above sea-level; 15 m. NE of Avignon; in N lat. 44° 3′ 16", E long. 5° 2'. Pop. in 1789, 9,400; in 1821, 9,099; in 1831, 9,817; in 1836, 9,224; and in 1846, 9,887. It is nearly triangular in form; and is surrounded by old walls in a good state of preservation. The streets are narrow, and many of them sinuous; but the houses are generally well-built. The principal edifices are the cathedral, a handsome Gothic structure and adorned with columns taken from the ancient temple of Diana at Venasque; a fine modern aqueduct; a Roman triumphal arch; and an hospital, the staircase of which is considered a chef-d'œuvre. There are also several public halls, a college, a theatre, a public library containing 22,000 vols., and a valuable collection of medals and other objects of curiosity, a synagogue, &c. C. possesses important manufactories of soap and nitric acid; distilleries of brandy, spirits of wine, and essences; dye-works, tanneries, a cotton spinning-mill, and 4 printing establishments. Its commerce, which is very active, consists in olive-oil, silk, madder, wine, spirits, nitric acid, saffron, almonds and other fruits, wax, honey, truffles, and clover and lucerne seed. Fairs are held here twice a-year, and a market weekly.—This town, the Carpentoractum of the Romans, is of great antiquity. It was the cap. of the Memini, and subsequently of the Comtat Venaissin. In the 3d cent. it became the seat of the bishopric of C.
CARPENZANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 9 m. SSE of Cosenza. Pop. 1,500. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.
CARPERBY-CUM-THORSBY, a township in the parish of Aysgarth, N. R. of Yorkshire, 81 m. WNW of Middleham. Area 3,460 acres. Pop. in 1841, 354.

CARPI, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Verona, district and 6 m. SE of Legnano, on the r. bank of the Adige. Pop. 1,200. It is surrounded by walls and defended by a castle. The French were here defeated by the Imperialists in 1701.—Also a town of the duchy and district and 10 m. N of Modena, and 12 m. SW of La Mirandola, on a canal of the same name. Pop. 6,500. It is defended by ancient walls and a fortress; and contains a cathedral and an episcopal seminary. It has some silk spinning-mills, and fairs are held twice a-year.

CARPIGNANO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 12 m. WNW of Novara, near the l. bank of the Sesia. Pop. 2,300. It has an annual fair.— Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra di Otranto, district and 17 m. SE of Lecce. Pop. 1,131.
CARPINETI, a village of Modena, in the district and 15 m. SSW of Reggio, near the source of the Tresinaro. It has an annual fair.

CARPINETO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 15 m. W of Frosinone.—Also a village of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Citra, district and 13 m. SW of Il Vasto, and 29 m. SE of Chieti. Pop. 1,014.
CARPINO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanate, district and 27 m. ENE of San-Severo, on an affluent and to the SE of Lake Varano. Pop. 6,061. It contains three churches.
CARPINONE, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario in the prov. of Sannio or Molise, 6 m. E of Isernia, and 19 m. W of Campobasso. Pop. 2,836.
CARPINTERO (SIERRA DEL), a mountain of New Granada, 7 m. SE of Cape la Vela.

CARPIO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 17 m. ENE of Cordova, and 5 m. NNW of Bujalance, on the l. bank of the Guadalquiver. Pop. 2,696. It is supposed to have been founded by the Romans. It contains a parish church, an hospital, and several convents.

CARPIO (EL), a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 24 m. W of Toledo, and 12 m. SW of Torrijos, in a mountainous district near the r. bank of the Tagus. Pop. 2,488.—Also a town of Leon, in the prov. and 32 m. SSW of Valladolid, in the partido and 8 m. WSW of Medina del Campo, in a district watered by the Minimes. Pop. 941. It contains a parish church and a custom-house.

CARQUEFOU, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, arrond. of Nantes. The cant. comprises 5 com., and in 1831 contained 7,164 inhabitants.—The village is at some distance from the l. bank of the Erdre, 7 m. NNE of Nantes. Pop. 2,626.
CARQUIN (Porto), a port of Peru, in the prov. of Lima, 50 m. NNW of Chancay, at the mouth of a small stream, on a shallow bay.

CARRA, a lake in co. Mayo. Its main body extends WSW 33 m., with a mean breadth of about 1 m. along the mutual boundary of the baronies of C. and Kilmain; and an arm 31 m. long, and rather less than a 1 m. in mean breadth, goes off from the middle of the W side. The lake is profusely isleted, has an intricate outline, and communicates by a brief river-run from the S end with Loch Mask. "As a sheet of water," says the author of the Wild Sports of the West, "nothing can be more beautiful than Lough C. Everything that the painter delights to fancy may be here realized. Islands and peninsulas, with rich overhanging woods, a boundless range of mountain-masses in the distance, ruins in excellent keeping, all form a splendid study for the artist's pencil." In 1838, the summer and winter surfaceelevations of the lake above sea-level were respectively 67 and 72 ft.
CARRACA. See CARACA.
CARRACEDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 70 m. WNW of Leon, and 5 m. SE of Villafranca, on the l. bank of the Qua.

CARRAGH, or CARRA (THE), a rivulet and two lakes on the N side of the great peninsula which extends between Dingle bay and the Kenmare river, in co. Kerry. The rivulet rises on the N side of Coomenagh, and has a course of about 12 m. chiefly N to Castlemain bay. Its basin is a chain of romantie mountain-glens; the chief attractions of which belong to two lacustrine expansions of the rivulet,the Loughs C., -which lakes extend upward from a point 21 m. above the mouth of the rivulet; they jointly have a length of nearly 3 m., and are connected by a brief run of the stream. They lie within 13 m. of Killarney.
CARRAMUNGALUM, a village of Hindostan, in the northern Circars, about 22 m. SE of Ryaguddi. In the vicinity are ruins of several ancient edifices.

CARRANCA, a town and parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the road from Baependi to São - João - d'Elrei. Pop., chiefly agricultural, 4,000. The district is watered by the Angai and Capivari, tributaries of the Rio Verde. In the vicinity is a mountain-range, from the gloomy aspect of which the district takes its name.
CARRANQUE, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 29 m. N of Toledo, partido and 4 m. NW of Illescas.
CARRAN-TUAL, the loftiest of the grandlyfeatured mountains called MacGillicuddy's Reeks, in co. Kerry. The alt. of its summit above sea-level is 3,410 ft.

CARRANZA, a valley of Spain, in Biscay, in the prov. and 23 m. W of Bilboa and partido of Valma- been described in a paper by Professor Hoffmann, II. * CAR seda. It is 6 m. in length, and about equal in breadth; and is watered by a small river which is formed of several mountain streams, and which affords good water-power. The valley produces little grain, but pastures large herds of sheep; and the surrounding mountains abound with medicinal plants, and contain a mine of antimony. Pop. 2,316.
CARRANZA (Point), a low headland of Chile, in the dep. of Maule, in S lat. 35° 35'. Extending to a considerable distance around each side is a reef of the same name.
CARRAPARI, or CARAPARI, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. and 150 m. ESE of Tarija, on an affluent of the Itau.
CARRAPATEIRA (PUNTA DA), a promontory of Portugal, on the W coast of the prov. of Algarve, in N lat. 37° 13'.

CARRA'RA, a town and district of Italy, which, with the duchy of Massa, forms the duchy of Massa- Carrara, an appanage of the duke of Modena. The district of C., amounting to about 30 sq. m., with a pop. of about 12,000, is bounded on the N by Tuscany; on the E by the duchy of Massa; on the S and SW by the Mediterranean; and on the W and NW by the Sardinian prov. of Lunigiana, and Modena. Its breadth from the coast to the summit of Monte Sagro, to the NE of the town of C., is about 8 m.—The town of C., famous for its marble-quarries, is 4 or 5 m. NNW of Massa, separated from it by a high hill, and lying in a narrow valley hemmed round with lofty and rugged mountains, barren of verdure or trees, but yielding from their bowels a rich revenue to the inhabitants. It is an ill-built, unsightly town, containing a pop. of 6,000; and there are 13 villages around it, about a mile apart from each other, containing an equal pop., all engaged in working the quarries. There is an academy of sculpture in the town, which has produced several good artists. The statuary marble is quarried at the elevation of several hundred feet above the town, and at the distance of about 13 m. The marble lies in strata at an inclination of about 60° from the horizon. It is snowy white, and of a closer texture than almost any other marble, owing to which it receives a finer surface and is more durable. The beds are from 1 to 4 ft. in thickness, alternating with beds from two to three inches thick containing tale, asbestos, steatite, and crystals of sulphuret-of-iron. The statuary marble is not found above a certain elevation in the hills, but an inferior kind exists several hundred feet above it. The dimensions of the blocks are of course limited, and the largest mass ever raised contained from 400 to 500 cubic feet. Two-thirds of all the marble got at C. is sent to England; Rome and France take comparatively little. Many of the blocks are sawn on the spot into slabs by water-mills moving a number of parallel saws, by which a large block is cut at one operation into ten or a dozen slabs. There are no less than 150 quarries of marble in the mountains of C., and they are all the property of private individuals. The town is becoming rich from its large commerce in marble, and the workmen earn high wages. The value of the marble annually exported is 500,000 francs. The number of workmen employed in the quarries and in working up the marble is about 1,200. The marble mountains of C. are situated in the NW part of the Alpe Apuana, a mountain-group completely detached from the Apennines, and extending from SE to NW about 13 m., with an average breadth of 6 m. The highest summits of this chain, the Pizzo d'Uccello and the Pania delle Broce, are not quite 5,800 Parisian ft. above sea-level. The geology of this region has which is translated in the Edin. New Phil. Journal, no. 41.

CARRAROE, a small group of mountains on the W border of the co. of Tyrone, 5 m. N by E of Lough Derg, and 9 m. ENE of Donegal. The principal summit, Brandy-hill, has an alt. above sea-level of 1,024 ft.
CARRASCAL, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. NNE of Segovia, partido and 8 m. NW of Sepielveda, near the r. bank of the Duranton.
CARRASCAL-DEL-OBISPO, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 24 m. SW of Salamanca, and 6 m. S of Matella.
CARRASCOSA-DEL-CAMPO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. E of Cuenca, partido and 12 m. SSE of Huete. Pop. 1,940.
CARRASCOSA - DE - HENARES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 25 m. NNE of Guadalajara, and partido of Brihuega.
CARRASCOSA-DEL-TAJO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 38 m. E of Guadalajara, partido and 8 m. SSE of Cifuentes.
CARRASCOSILLA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 35 m. WNW of Cuenca, partido and 6 m. ESE of Huete, near the l. bank of the Rio-Huete.
CARREGHOVA, a township in the p. of Llan-y- Mynech, Denbighshire, 51 m. SSW of Oswestry, in Salop, on the Vernieu, and near the Montgomery canal. Pop. in 1841, 388.
CARREIRA ISLES, a group of islets off the NW coast of Spain, to the N of the entrance of Arosa bay, Galicia, in N lat. 42° 30′, and W long. 8° 55′.
CARREIRA (SAN PELAYO), a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 70 m. from Corunna, partido and 22 m. S of San Mastrio de Noya, at the N side of the entrance of Arosa bay.
CARRENA, a town of Spain, in Asturias, in the prov. and 58 m. ESE of Oviedo, partido and 20 m. SE of Santa-Maria-de-Cangas-de-Onis.

CARRENTEEL, a parish, containing a v. of the same name, in co. Tyrone, Ulster. It lies along the N side of the river Blackwater, and on the S frontier of the county. Area 13,431 acres. Pop. in 1831, 7,459; in 1841, 7,903. The v. is <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. NE of Aughnacloy, on the road to Dungannon. Pop. 110.
CARREPUIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Roye. Pop. 310. It contains a bell-foundry.
CARRETAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 56 m. ENE of Goyaz. Mining and the rearing of cattle form the chief occupations of the inhabitants. It was originally called Pedro Terceiro.
CARRETO, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. and 130 m. E of Panama, on the W side of the gulf of Darien.
CARREUC (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Côtes-du-Nord, cant. of Moncontour. Pop. 1,144.

CARRIACOU, or Cariaco, an island of the West Indies, in the group of the Grenadines, in the gov. and 15 m. NE of the island of Grenada, in N lat. 12° 28', and W long. 61° 29'. It is 7 m. in length, by <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in breadth; and in 1832 contained 3,823 inhabitants, of whom 3,360 were slaves. In 1844 the pop. was estimated at 3,847, including 200 located at Petite Martinique, an islet 2 m. to the NE. The general aspect and formation of the island is mountainous. The principal productions of the soil, which is generally fertile, are sugar and cotton. Of the former, 2,009,200 lbs. were manufactured in 1825; 1,343,340 lbs. in 1838; and 971,120 lbs. in 1847. The quantity of cotton grown is now very limited. Maize and ground-provisions are extensively grown. CAR This island frequently suffers severely from extreme drought. On the SW side of the island is a bay which forms a good port, and on which Hillsborough, the chief town, is situated.
CARRICHES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 20 m. WNW of Toledo, partido and 7 m. SW of Torrijos.

CARRIBEAN SEA. See CARIBBEAN SEA. CARRICAL, CARICALA, or KARIKAL, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, principality and 57 m. ENE of Tanjore, at the embouchure of a river of the same name, a branch of the Caveri, and 75 m. S of Pondicherry, in N lat. 10° 55', and E long. 79° 55'. It is of considerable extent; and contains a church and several Hindu temples; but its fortifications are entirely destroyed. It possesses, however, important manufactories of cotton-fabrics and large building docks; and notwithstanding the disadvantages of its port, the entrance to which is impeded by a sand-bar, has an active commerce.—The territory of C. comprises 4 maganoms or districts and 113 villages, with a pop. amounting in 1836 to 44,191; of whom 40 were whites, and 44,105 natives. It is watered by several branches of the Caveri, which render the soil extremely fertile. Its principal production is rice, which, with the produce of the salines on the coast, forms the chief article of local export.—The town and territory of C. were ceded to the French in 1739 by the rajah of Tanjore. In 1803 the town was taken by the English, but was restored by treaty to the French in 1814.

CARRICK, the S district of Ayrshire. It is bounded on the N by Kyle, or Ayr proper; on the E by Dumfries-shire and the stewartry of Kirkcudbright; on the S by Wigton; and on the W by the Atlantic ocean. It comprehends the parishes of Ballantrae, Barr, Colmonell, Dailly, Girvan, Kirkmichael, Kirkoswald, Maybole, and Straiton. It is about 32 m. in length, by 20 m. in breadth, and its superficial area may be estimated in round numbers at 300,000 acres. Pop. in 1831, 25,536; in 1841, 28,460, of whom 21,766 were natives of the co. Its surface is hilly; and the name may have originated in the Gaelic carraig, 'a rock.' The mountains, especially on the NW, seem to be a continuation of that great ridge which, extending from the confines of England, through the counties of Selkirk, Peebles, Lanark, and Dumfries, meets the Western ocean between the districts of Carrick and Kyle. In the valleys between the hills, and along the sea-shore, are many stripes of level ground of a fine clay or loamy soil. The chief rivers are the Girvan and the Stinchar; the Doon forms its E boundary. There are several lakes; and a great part of the country is still covered with natural wood.

CARRICK, a parish in co. Kildare, 3 m. NW by W of Castle-Carbery. Area 5,196 acres. Pop. in 1831, 604; in 1841, 552.—Also a parish in co. Westmeath, 51 m. S by W of Mullingar. Area 2,957 acres. Pop. 532. The surface extends along the E side of Lough Ennel.—Also a parish in co. Louth, 3 m. SE of Dunleer. Area 8264 acres. Pop. 302. — Also a parish 24 m. W of Wexford, in co. Wexford. Area 3,009 acres. Pop. 1,251. It lies along the r. bank of the Slaney, and of the upper part of Wexford harbour; and is celebrated in connection with Carrick-castle, the first that was built by the Anglo- Normans in Ireland .- Also a village in the p. of Bannow, co. Wexford. Pop. 229.

CARRICK-A-REDE, an insulated basaltic rock, in co. Antrim, in the immediate vicinity of the v. of Ballintoy, 12 m. W of Kenbane-head. It consists of rudely prismatic basalt; has an extreme alt. of 350 ft.; and is so faced round with perpendicular cliffs as-except at one small creek, and even there but at particular periods-to be altogether inaccessible from the sea. It is only 60 ft. distant from the high, mural, basaltic cliff of the mainland. A flying bridge, 60 ft. long and 84 ft. high, connects it with the mainland. This bridge consists of two strong parallel cables fastened to rings in the solid rock, and bearing a boarded pathway, and of a rope 3 ft. higher, which guides the hand, and assists the equilibrium of the passenger.

CARRICKBEG, a town in the p. of Kilmoleran, co. Waterford, on the river Suir, directly opposite to Carrick-on-Suir; and, in every thing except political distribution, part of that town. Pop. in 1831, 2,704; in 1841, 2,680.
CARRICKDUFF, a village in the p. of Barragh, co. Carlow. Pop. 243.

CARRICKFERGUS, a parish, commensurate with the county of the town of the same name, on the southern border of co. Antrim. Length and breadth, each 4 m.; area 16,700 acres. Pop. in 1831, 8,706; in 1841, 9,379. Families in 1841 employed chiefly in agriculture, 669; in manufactures and trade, 820; in other pursuits, 376. The surface varies from alluvial plain to lofty mountain; presents pleasing transitions in its diversities, and abounds in the attractions both of close landscape, and of distant and brilliant prospect. An upland ridge, from 500 to 1,000 ft. high, and upwards of a mile in mean breadth, extends ENE from end to end of the parish, and occupies all the northern half of the central district. Most of this upland is moor and bog; and 2,430 statute acres are borough common. The highest ground is near the western boundary, and commands an extensive and singularly imposing view.

CARRICKFERGUS, a post, market, and seaport town, a parliamentary borough, and the assize-town of co. Antrim, on the southern border of the cognominal parish, 93 m. S of Larne; 8 m. NE by N of Belfast; and 88 m. N by E of Dublin.-Excepting a street, or partially edificed road, which extends from the W end north-westward along the thoroughfare toward Ballinure, the whole town stretches close along Belfast lough, in the direction of ENE. The town consists of the ancient city or walled town, in the centre; the Irish quarter, on the W; and the Scotch quarter, on the E. The walls still to a great extent exist, and may all be distinctly traced. The streets are irregularly disposed, and of various breadth; yet, excepting the principal street, of about 150 yards in length, the open area around the castle, and the Joymount-court promenade, which goes out to the Scotch quarter, the whole are squeezed together in the suffocating density of oriental city pressure. The Scotch quarter is occupied chiefly by fishermen, and had its name from a Scotch colony of the same craft of the present inhabitants' ancestors, who arrived about the year 1665, from Galloway and Argyleshire, and were driven from their native land by the violence of prelatic persecution. The whole town, with the exception of a mere sprinkling of houses, has a mere village character, - consisting of thatched cabins and straggling suburban abodes; and the densest parts of it are so crowded, irregular, and untidy, as to produce on a stranger an irksome and repulsive effect. Most of the houses are built of stone; some are of brick; and ancient ones, which have now nearly all disappeared, consisted of bricks in frames of oak .- The parish-church crowns a rising ground, and occupies a somewhat open area, in a central situation. It measures 132 ft. in length, but is of very unequal breadth, narrow, and inconveniently low .- The castle, though comparatively small, and defaced by modern incongruous additions, possesses great interest at once as an architectural object, as a conspicuous feature in multitudinous groupings of land- town, the capital of co. Leitrim, and formerly a par- # CAR scape, and as the scene of various important historical events. Its site is a rocky peninsula, about 30 ft. high, shelving considerably to the land, washed on three sides by the bay, and entirely occupied by the works of the fortress.-The county court-house and gaol constitute one suite of buildings, and were erected in 1777-9, at the cost of £21,785.—The woollen manufacture was once of some importance in C., but has declined. The cotton trade belongs more to the parish than to the town. Linen weaving and bleaching occupy similar prominence as in most of the towns of eastern Ulster. Leather is manufactured to a considerable amount. Within the town are a cotton manufactory, a flax-mill, and a distillery. The fishery of the bay affords employment to a large proportion of the inhabitants, and yields abundance of cod, lobsters, and oysters. The import and export trade was at one time of note, and promised to become of large importance, but has, for generations past, been almost completely destroyed by the rivalry and more advantageous situation of Belfast. Such imports as exist consist of coals, iron, timber, bark, salt, slates, and miscellaneous goods; and the exports amount to about 10,000 tons per annum, and consist of black cattle, grain, alabaster, and part of the produce of the manufactories. There are two piers,—the one enclosing the town-harbour immediately SW of the castle, and the other situated in the Scotch quarter. Both are dry at low water; and the former has depth at high water for vessels of 100 tons.—Excepting the area and precincts of the castle, and the site of the county court-house and gaol, the whole of the lands occupied by the town, and of those within the limits of the parish, constitute a jurisdiction separate from that of the co. of Antrim. In 1841, the constituency amounted to 1,326. Area of the town 129 acres. Pop. in 1841, 3,885. Houses 614. History.] The site of C. castle is said to have been a stronghold of the Dalriads, and to have obtained the name of Carraig- Feargusa, or 'the rock of Feargus,' from a king of that name who was drowned in its vicinity. Old documents usually mention the place under some corruption of this name, or under the cognate designation of Knockfergus. The De Lacey family became possessors of this fortress. About 1387, the town was entirely burnt by the Scotch. During the whole of Tyrone's rebellion, Carrickfergus was the head-quarters of the English forces in the North. In 1642, General Monroe, at the head of 4,000 Scottish auxiliaries, landed to assist against the Irish rebels, and took possession of C. and its castle. In 1648, General Monk surprised and seized Monroe, and took possession for the English parliament. In 1649, the Earl of Inchiquin captured the castle for the king, and made Monk prisoner; and, toward the close of the same year, the castle surrendered to Sir Charles Coote. In 1668, Lord Iveagh held the place for James II.; and next year, the Duke of Schomberg sailed into the bay, saw the outworks of the castle burnt on apprehension of a siege, landed at Bangor, and marched round by Belfast, invested C., and, after making some breaches on its fortifications, and encountering an obstinate resistance, became master of the town. In 1760, Commodore Thurst, with about 1,000 Frenchmen, landed at C., and captured the town.

CARRICKMACROSS, or Magheross, a parish, containing a town of the same name, in co. Monaghan. Area 16,702 acres. Pop. in 1831, 12,600; in 1841, 13,444. The centre of the parish contains the watershed between the E and the NW seas, and forms the outer rim of the basin of the Louth Lagan on the E, and of the remote affluents of the Erne on the W.—The market and post-town of C. stands on the mail-road from Dublin to Londonderry, 4 m. N by W of the point at which the counties of Monaghan, Meath, and Louth meet, and 92 m. S of Castleblaney. It consists principally of one street, is wellbuilt, and has a comparatively handsome appearance. Pop. in 1831, 2,979; in 1841, 1,997. Houses 345.
CARRICKMINES, a village in co. Dublin, on the road from Dundrum to Bray, nearly midway between them; and 13 m. from the sea, on a rivulet which falls into Killeeny bay.
CARRICK-ON-SHANNON, a post and market

CARRON, a village in the p. of Larbert, in Stirlingshire, on the N bank of the Carron, about 3 m. from its entrance into the Forth, and 2 m. N of the town of Falkirk. It is celebrated as the most extensive iron-foundry in Europe; though, of late years, a greater quantity of pig-iron has been manufactured at some other works. These works employ about 2,000 workmen. There are 5 blast or smelting-furnaces, 4 cupola-furnaces, and 20 air-furnaces; with mills for grinding fire-clay, boring cylinders, and grinding and polishing cast metal; and besides the machinery which is driven by water, there is a steamengine of 90 horse-power, which is used entirely in the production of blast. All kinds of cast-iron goods are manufactured here; not only instruments of war, such as cannon, mortars and carronades, shot and shells, but implements of agriculture, of the arts, and for domestic use, pipes, boilers, ovens, vats, pots, grates, and smith and machinery of all kinds. Two kinds of ore are employed in these works together, in regular proportions. The first is a species of decomo posed hæmatites brought from Cumberland, which stains the hand of a blood-red colour; the second is the common argillaceous ironstone, of a yellowish brown colour, and of a rocky hardness. From the proper proportions of these ores an iron is procured, equal, if not superior, to the sable iron imported from Russia. The C. works were first projected by Dr. Roebuck of Sheffield, in 1760; are carried on by a chartered company, with a capital of £150,000 sterling, divided into 600 shares, which are now in a few hands. The company hold and work for themselves extensive mines of iron, coal, and lime, besides possessing an immense stock of all materials requisite for carrying on the establishment, and about 20 vessels for exporting their manufactures to London and other ports, and for conveying ironstone and limestone to their works.

CARRON, a small but remarkable river in Stirlingshire. It rises in or near the Carron bog, and falls into the Forth at Grangemouth, about 3 m. NE of Falkirk, after a course of 14 m. At Larbert a dam is built across the river, which supplies the great reservoir at the Carron works. About half-a-mile from the river, and the same distance from Falkirk, a battle was fought between the Scots under Sir William Wallace, and the English under Edward I., in 1298. Not far distant from the same spot, the second battle of Falkirk was fought in 1745, betwixt Prince Charles Edward and the troops of the family of Hanover, in which the latter were defeated.—Also a rivulet in Nithsdale, which rises at the foot of the Lowther hills, and after a course of about 9 m. through the parish of Durisdeer, falls into the Nith at Carronfoot, on the road from Thornhill to Sanguhar .- Also a small river in Ross-shire, which flows in a SW direction, through a chain of small lakes, and falls into Loch-Carron.—Also a rivulet in Kincardineshire, which rises in Glenbervie, and falls into the sea at Stonehaven, forming a fine natural harbour.
CARRONSHORE, a village in Stirlingshire, partly in the p. of Larbert, and partly in that of Bothkennar, 2 m. below Carron-works. Pop. 838.
CARRU, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 7 m. NNE of Mondovi, on an affluent of the Tenaro. Pop. 3,800. Fairs are held twice a-year.

CARRY, or CARRI-LE-ROUET, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Martigues, on the Mediterranean, 22 m. SW of Aix. Pop. 356. It has extensive tunny-fisheries, and a small port.
CARRYGLASS, a village in the p. of Mogeela, co. Cork, 1 m. W of Tallow. Pop. 262.
CARR-Y-VILLAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. CAR and 27 m. SW of Leon, partido and 5 m. SE of Astorga, on the r. bank of the Tuerto.
CARS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and 2 m. E of Blaye. Pop. 1,640. Wine is grown in the environs.
CARSHALTON, or Casehorton, a parish of Surrey, 11 m. SSW of London. Area 2,680 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,228. It was formerly a market-town.
CARSINGTON, a parish of Derbyshire, 2 m. W of Wirksworth, and S of the Cromford and High Peak railway. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. in 1841, 235.
CARSIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. and 6 m. from Bernay. Pop. 683. It contains manufactories of linen.

CARSOLI, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, district and 21 m. WNW of Avezzano. Pop. 1,673. It is built on the declivity of a hill surmounted by a ruined castle; and contains 4 churches, a convent, and a customhouse.—The plain of C. is extensive, verdant, and well-cultivated. The site of the ancient Carsoli or Carseoli is now covered with vineyards.
CARSPACH, or Carolsbach, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, cant. of Altkirch. Pop. 1,064.
CARSPHAIRN, a large upland parish in Kirkcudbrightshire, intersected by the Deugh water. Pop. in 1801, 496; in 1841, 790.

CARSTAIRS, a parish in the upper ward of Lanarkshire, intersected by the river Mouse. Pop. in 1801, 899; in 1841, 950, of whom 340 were in the v. of C., 3 m. W of Carnwath. The Glasgow branch of the Caledonian railway joins the Edinburgh branch in this p. at a point about \( \frac{1}{2} \) m. S of Carnwath on the l. bank of the Clyde.
CARSWELL, a hamlet in the p. of Buckland, Berks. Pop. in 1841, 93.

CART, two rivers in Renfrewshire, one of which rises in Eaglesham moors, passes Pollockshaws and Paisley, and falls into the Clyde after a course of about 22 m. The other rises in Castle-Semple loch, and joins the White cart at Inchinnan bridge.
CARTAGENA. See CARTHAGENA.

CARTAGO, a town of Costa Rica, on a river of the same name, 60 m. from its entrance into the gulf of Nicoya, about equidistant from the shore of the Caribbean sea, and 160 m. SE of Nicaragua. It was formerly flourishing, and possessed at the commencement of the present cent. a pop. estimated at 26,000. In 1841 it was devastated by an earthquake. -Also a town of New Grenada, in the dep. of Cundinamarca, prov. and 165 m. NNE of Popayan, on the Vieja, near its confluence with the Cauca, at an alt. of 3,150 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 4° 44′ 50″. Pop. 5,500. Its streets are straight and spacious, and it contains a handsome square. Its situation, at the junction of the roads from Santa-Fe-de-Bogota on the E, and from the more southerly districts of New Granada to Novita on the W and Citara, render it a commercial entrepot of great importance; and it possesses also an active local trade in coffee, cacao, and tobacco. The climate of the locality is warm, but dry and salubrious.
CARTAGO (PORTO), a harbour on the E coast of Costa Rica, at the mouth of the Malina, in N lat. 10° 5'.

CARTAMA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 16 m. W of Malaga, partido and 15 m. SSE of Alora, at the foot of a hill on the summit of which is an old castle, and near the r. bank of the Guadalgore. Pop. 1,993. It contains some Roman remains, a church, and a custom-house.

CARTAYA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 9 m. WNW of Huelva, in a flat district, near the l. bank of the Piedra, and about 4 m. from the sea. Pop. 4,097. It is of considerable antiquity; and contains a church, a school, an hospital, and several convents.
CARTELEGUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Blaye. Pop. 1,101.

CARTER, a county in the state of Tennessee, U.S., comprising a surface of 540 sq. m., drained by Watauga river, a branch of the Holston. In the E and S it is rocky and mountainous. Pop. in 1830, 6,414; and in 1840, 5,372, of whom 4,998 were whites, 352 slaves, and 22 free coloured. Cap., Elizabethtown. -Also a county in the state of Kentucky, generally hilly, and drained by Little Sandy river and Tygart's creek. Pop. in 1840, 2,905, of whom 2,711 were whites, 186 slaves, and 8 free coloured. Cap., Grayson.

CARTERET, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Barneville, on the English channel, at the mouth of the Gerfleur, and 22 m. from Valognes. Pop. 513. It has a good roadstead for large vessels, and a small port, the trade of which, especially with the island of Jersey, is very active. Its exports consist in pigs, sheep, fowls, eggs, butter, wine, legumes and grain. Its principal imports are coal and timber.—On Cape C. is a lighthouse, exhibiting an intermittent light elevated 262 ft. above high water.

CARTERET, a county in the state of North Carolina, U. S., comprising an area of 600 sq. m., stretching along the Atlantic, from which it is separated by narrow ridges of rock and sand, on one of which is Cape Look-out. It is flat and marshy, and its soil sandy. Pop. in 1830, 6,607; in 1840, 6,591, of whom 5,087 were whites, 1,360 slaves, and 144 free coloured. The cap. is Beaufort.
CARTERET, an islet of the South Pacific, in St. George's channel, between the islands of New Ireland and New Britain, in S lat. 4° 40', and E long. 152° 18′.

CARTERET ISLANDS, a group of islets in the South Pacific, in the Salomon group, in Slat. 4° 45', and E long. 155° 15'. It derives its name from the English navigator by whom it was discovered in 1767.
CARTER-FELL, one of the Cheviot range, in the p. of Southdean in Roxburghshire. Alt. 2,020 ft. The Jed has its source on the N side of this hill; and the Northumbrian Tyne on the S side.
CARTES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. SW of Santander, partido and 2 m. SW of Torrelavega, near the l. bank of the Besaya.

CARTHAGE, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 41 m. NW of Augusta. It is watered by Webb's river, a branch of the Androscoggin, and contains several ponds. Pop. in 1840, 522.—Also a village of Wilna township, Jefferson co., in the state of New York, 152 m. NW of Albany, on the N side of Black river. Pop. 600.—Also a village of Mill Creek township, Hamilton co., in the state of Ohio, 8 m. N of Cincinnati. It has an annual co. fair.—Also a village, cap. of Moore co., in the state of North Carolina, 3 m. E of M'Lennon's creek, and 79 m. SW of Raleigh.—Also a village, cap. of Leake co., in the state of Mississippi, 57 m. NE of Jackson. -Also a village, cap. of Smith co., in the state of Tennessee, 52 m. E of Nashville, on the N side of Cumberland river, at the junction of the Cany-fork creek. Pop. in 1840, 600. - Also a township of Athens co., in the state of Ohio. It is hilly, but moderately fertile. Pop. 737. - Also a village of Brighton township, Monroe co., in the state of New York, 2 m. N of Rochester, at the lower falls of the Genesee river, and 5 m. above its entrance into Lake Ontario. It contains about 60 dwellings. - Also a village, cap. of Hancock co., in the state of Illinois, 9 m. from the Mississippi, between Bear and Long creeks, and 106 m. WNW of Springfield. In the vicinity is abundance of timber and coal. * CAR

CARTHAGE, the once celebrated capital of a great country, now distinguished only by its cisterns, the remains of some amphitheatres, and an aqueduct, built on a high promontory forming the Wextremity of Tunis bay, now called Cape Carthage, in N lat. 36° 51′ 30", 10 m. NE of Tunis. "Covered," says M. Flachenacker, "with pediments and capitals, fragments of bas-reliefs, and columns of marble and of porphyry, and numerous and vast cisterns, which time has scarcely touched,-that immense solitude which bore, of old, the name of Carthage, is broken by no other sound than the monotonous song of the half-naked Arab, who feeds his flock amid the wrecks of temples and of palaces, and to whom Hannibal and St. Louis are alike unknown. Over this immense necropolis presides the solitary tower in which St. Louis died, to this day called, by a misnomer, 'the Tomb of St. Louis,' and recalling to the imagination the stormy scenes of the Crusaders. It seems certain, from such slender indications as remain, that C. stood on the tongue of land formed by the Mediterranean on one side, and the lake on the other-that is to say, upon a space occupying nearly 3 leagues in extent—a vast plain intersected by small hills, amid which lie scattered the numerous vestiges of the superb city that held the sceptre of the seas. The city itself, properly so called-which is said to have been surrounded by a triple enclosure, and capable of containing from 20,000 to 25,000 fighting men-stretched from the foot of the hill on which the citadel Byrsa, now Beursak, stood, N to the environs of Marsa-the place where the consuls have their countryhouses-and in a S direction to the lake, which even covers a portion of its faubourgs. The best proof which I can offer of this last assertion is, that when the sky is clear, and the waters of the lake unruffled, architectural remains are visible, on which the keels that make the passage from Goletta to Tunis are frequently impeded when they have steered a wrong course, or are too deeply laden. In this vast space, which presents the form of an immense triangle, whose apex was the citadel, was included the site now occupied by the modern village of Malga, constructed over a multitude of cisterns, within which the Arabs have made their homes, and find magnificent stables for their horses and cattle. These it is probable are the elephants' stables spoken of by Lady Montagu, and which M. de Chateaubriand states himself to have discovered in the subterranean chambers of a miserable village lying at the foot of the ancient hill of Byrsa, and conjectured by him to be the Tens of Lady Montagu. These places, however, could never have had the destination which she supposes; because, in the first place, they are too low to have been the stables of animals so enormous, and, in the second, their immense number (the entire plain being covered with them) altogether excludes such idea. It is more rational to suppose that they served as lodgings for the troops, or perhaps for the poor amongst the population of C., as, in our days, we see the Bedouins of Malga, Oudenah, Menzell, Sidi-Rhajes, and other places, occupying similar subterranean abodes, which offer a residence more cool and commodious than tents." The cothon, or port, which is placed by Shaw to the NW of C., Flachenacker gives his reasons for laying down at exactly the opposite point of the compass. "I have examined," he says, "not once, but seven or eight times, the NW coast, and have never discovered the slightest vestige indicating the site of a port. I have found only the ruins of a few country-houses along the shore, in which, with all the inclination in the world, it is impossible to find any trace either of mole or jetty, or anything else appertaining to a port. On the SE, on the contrary, that is to say, along the whole extent of the coast which borders the roadstead, and which stretches from Sidi-Ben-Said, a modern village built on the cape of C., to Goletta, numerous indications of a port are met with at every step. Another reason, which seems to me decisive, for placing the port in this part of the coast is, that the Carthaginians, the most maritime people of antiquity, well knowing the violence of the NW wind, which blows, during a large portion of the year, on these coasts,-and is known in our day under the name of the mistral,-would never have dug, at great labour, a port on the NW coast, exposed to this very wind, when they had, on the opposite side of the tongue of land on which they had built their city, a wide, and admirable roadstead, with a natural port, where the mistral and the NE wind lose a great portion of their violence,—arrested, as they are, the one by Cape C., and the other by the Cape Zafran."

CARTHAGENA, or CARTAGENA, a sea-port of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, in 37° 36' N lat., and 1° W long.; 6 leagues W of Cabo-de-Palos, the SE cape of Spain, and 27 m. S by E of Murcia. It was founded by Asdrubal, in the year 228 B. C., and was at first called New Carthage; but it received from the Romans the name of Carthago Spartiari, on account, it is supposed, of the great quantity of spartium or broom which grew in its vicinity. It was the capital of the Roman province called Provincia Carthaginiensis. In 421, it was taken and destroyed by the Vandals; but it was entirely rebuilt by Philip II. for the sake of its excellent harbour.—The town stands on the declivity of a hill, and is separated from the harbour by a small plain. On the S and W are high mountains and barren rocks; towards the N and E it communicates with an extensive valley of a finely varied surface and fertile soil. The streets are spacious, but few of them are good. The houses are in general flat-roofed, and sufficiently commodious. On the top of a hill commanding the city, there is a castle which is in a state of decay; and on the adjoining heights are walls of considerable strength erected for the defence of the harbour, arsenal, and dock-yard. The public buildings are not remarkable. They are chiefly the parade, which is raised on a regular plan, -the royal hospital is a large establishment intended for the reception of the sick both in the navy and army,-the cathedral, which is a miserable pile, and, since the bishop's see was removed to Murcia, has been degraded to the rank of a parish-church,—the convents, none of which deserve particular notice,the arsenal, a spacious square situated to the SW of the town, under the mountains, defenceless on the landside, but protected by cannon in its approach from the sea, -and the dock-yards, consisting of wet-docks, in which the men-of-war are moored each opposite to the door of its own magazine of stores, and of drydocks, so much infested with water oozing from the marshy soil, that they can never be clear of it. The harbour, however, is the best on the E coast of Spain, though now nearly deserted for that of Alicant. It is very spacious, and so deep that vessels may be moored close to the land; and, in one position in it, a large fleet may lie in the utmost security, completely out of the view of ships at sea, or even in the narrow part of the entrance to the harbour. Its outline, which is of the figure of a heart, is entirely formed by the hand of nature. On the E and W, high naked mountains rise steep from the water's edge; on the N, the low and narrow ridge of hills, on which the city stands, shuts out the view of the inland country. The entrance, which is defended by two redoubts, has the island Escombrera lying off it in such a way as to shelter it from the violence of the winds and waves; indeed, so perfectly is it protected by the high hills which surround it, that from the mole the entrance of the harbour and basin alone are visible, and even during a storm the water remains calm and unruffled. In the centre of the haven, however, in a line between the mouth and the mole-gate, there is a ledge of rocks only 5 ft. under water. There are schools at C. for teaching navigation, mathematics, experimental philosophy, drawing, fortification, hydrostatics, &c., but they are instituted for the benefit of pupils for the royal navy, and the public at large have no access to them. There is likewise a botanic garden on a small scale, where lectures are given on botany.—The pop. of C. is about 30,000; in 1786 it contained double that number. The most prevalent diseases are intermittent and rutrid fevers, occasioned, it is believed, by the proximity of the swamp of Almojar. In the years 1785 and 1786, the city lost by these diseases no fewer than 4,800 of its inhabitants.—Swinburne, who visited C. in 1775, says, that though there were several regiments here in garrison, besides engir ers and naval officers, he could scarcely imagine any thing so dull as the town of C. Laborde, indeed, gives a very different account of the place; but Ford's recent testimony harmonizes with that of Swinburne: "Ruin, neglect, and emptiness," he says, "prevail every where." In the immediate vicinity of C., diamonds, rubies, amethysts, and other precious stones are said to have been found; and there are still silver mines, though it is believed they are nearly exhausted. There are also in the environs four hot springs, and some traces of an alum mine; and at no great distance from the city is a mountain which furnishes the gypsum used for plaster. Saltpetre abounds throughout the whole country. C. has some extensive vineyards, from which are produced a luscious and thick wine, and also a sweet wine, which is delicate and agreeable, and said to be not inferior to the wine of Alicant. In the neighbourhood of C. spart or broom is still extensively cultivated. Anciently, as Pliny tells us, it furnished the common people with material for bedding, clothes, shoes, and fire; it is still used for making shoes, ships' cables, and mats for houses; and even a fine thread has been spun from it for the purpose of being weaved into cloth. Although not so much attended to as formerly, it is yet considered of sufficient importance to be an article of exportation, and as such is subjected to the operation of duties and monopolies. The fishery here is considerable, and is carried on both within the port and in the open sea. The fishing within the port belongs to a company established by royal charter. A good deal is consumed at home; but the greatest proportion is salted and sent to the foreign market. This branch of industry, as usual, is much discouraged by various injudicious regulations and unfair exactions on the part of government and its inferior officers. The most important and valuable article of commerce at C. is barilla, or potash, the quality of which is excellent, and of which great quantities are annually exported. The plants which yield this substance are all indigenous, and are particularly to be found in a swamp called Almojar to the E of the city. Government with its accustomed folly, has subjected the barilla to an oppressive tax, which operates very much against the manufacture and exportation of it. The articles exported from C., and the prov. of Murcia, consist of wheat, barley, Indian corn, oil, barilla, red pepper, bass-weed, raw and manufactured silk; all of which are produced in the prov. of Murcia. A considerable quantity of plain and cut glass is exported from this to the north of Spain. The principal articles of merchandise imported into C. consist of bacallao, or salt fish, sugar, cacoa, coffee, rice, tobacco, pepper, iron, cinnamon, soap, and brandy, from America, Biscay, Majorca, and Catalonia. A few articles of English and French manufactures are imported, such as hardwares, linens, and woollens, the consumption in this prov. being very limited. The trade of C. has been declining for the last thirty years, but it is during these last fifteen or twenty years that it has so much fallen off. Two causes have conspired to produce this decline; namely, first, the raging of an epidemical fever in 1804, 1810, 1811, and 1812, to which from 20,000 to 30,000 of the inhabitants fell victims, out of a population of 50,000; and, secondly, the abandonment of the naval arsenal, which is now in a state of decay. The commerce of this city is at present in a very depressed state, and with respect to an increase of trade, it is believed that it must gradually improve, as the mines in this neighbourhood are now taking a favourable turn, which, with proper management, will undoubtedly return considerable profits to the shareholders at large, and thus improve the commerce of C. All kinds of cotton goods, earthenware, glass ware, furniture, tobacco, snuff, lead mineral, quicksilver, sulphur, manufactured hides, ditto tin or pewter, books, every sort of embroidered manufacture, marble, gold and silver jewellery, &c., are absolutely prohibited to be imported. No prohibition exists there with respect to articles imported from countries not being the place of the growth or production; such articles are admitted upon the same terms, whether coming direct from the place of their production or from any other country. Spanish vessels enjoy privileges of importation; namely, all goods or merchandise in Spanish vessels upon importation pay a fixed percentage on the value of the article, but if the same article is imported in a foreign vessel, it pays a greater, and sometimes a half more duty. The trade of Eng- AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND land with C. is of comparative small value. In 1837, there arrived six vessels, importing only £240 value of goods, and sailing with produce of the country. In 1842, there arrived only three British vessels, two of which were from England, importing British goods valued at £19,404, and one from Jamaica. In the same year 13 New Granadian vessels of only 1,023 tons, arrived with assorted cargoes from Jamaica, and two Spanish vessels from the same place.

CARTHAGENA, or CARTAGENA, a province of New Granada, in the dep. of Magdalena. Its boundaries are the Caribbean sea on the NW and N; the river Saint Marthá on the E; on the S, the prov. of Antioquia; and the bay of Choco and the gulf of Darien on the W. A great part of this country is composed of mountains and forests; but there are also some fruitful valleys, which the natives call savannahs. Towards the N, a part of the surface is swampy, sandy, and, from the lowness of the ground, full of salt-water pools. The climate is remarkably hot and moist; and from May to December, the weather is almost continually rainy or tempestuous. Rice and Indian corn are extensively raised, but the circumstances which render the production of these so easy and certain, are unfavourable to the growth of wheat and the other grains of Europe. Of trees, the principal for size are, the caobo or acajou, the wood of which, beautiful, fragrant, and close in the texture, is employed in making canoes and other small vessels; the cedar, of two kinds, white and red, the last being the most esteemed; the maria, from which the maria oil is obtained; and the tree which distils the balsam of Tolu, so called from a village where it is found in the greatest abundance and of the best quality. Pine-apples, limes, melons, grapes, oranges, medlars, dates, papayas, cocoas, plantains, and bananas, are abundant. The mancanillo deserves particular notice. It grows to a large size; near the top its branches form a kind of crown; its wood is hard, of a yellowish cast, and exquisitely veined like marble; its fruit resembles an apple in shape, flavour, and colour, but contains a strong and deadly poison; and its white juice has the same quality to such a degree as to render it unsafe to work the wood until it be thoroughly dried. The guiacum and ebony trees are very common. There are extensive plantations of sugar-canes; and the cotton-tree grows spontaneously, but is also planted and cultivated. The cacoa-tree is chiefly found on the river Magdalena, and the fruit, both in size and goodness, is said to excel that of all other parts of the country, except Soconusca and Caracas, whose cacoa sells highest at Cadiz. Hides and tallow furnish a considerable article of commerce. Poultry, pigeons, partridges, and geese, are plentiful; and nothing can surpass the variety and beautiful plumage of the birds. Deer, wild boars, foxes, armadillos, squirrels, rabbits, and monkeys, are extremely numerous; there are also leopards and tigers which occasionally make great havoc among the cattle. Poisonous reptiles abound: the principal are snakes, coral-snakes, rattle-snakes, and the culibras de bejuco. Centipedes swarm, and are of monstrous size; scorpions are equally numerous, and of various kinds. -The principal rivers are the Magdalena, already mentioned, and the San Juan or Atrato, both of which are navigable.

CARTHAGENA, the capital of the province just de- | New Grenada, 220,509 lbs.; balsam of Tolu, 2,205 lbs.; scribed, is situated on a large and convenient bay of the same name, in N lat. 10° 25′ 48", W long. 75° 30', about 50 m. from the mouth of the Magdalena. It stands upon a sandy peninsula, and communicates with the continent by narrow necks of land and wooden bridges. The city and the suburbs are well laid out. The streets run in straight lines; are wide, uniform, and tolerably well-paved. The houses, which | most entirely to specie and bullion, which is conveyare mostly built of stone, have only one story above the ground-floor; their apartments are well-contrived; and their balconies and lattices are of wood, which, from the nature of the climate, is here preferable to iron for such purposes. The fortifications are constructed after the modern manner, lined with freestone, and of considerable strength. Besides a precinct and bastions, there is a half-moon which defends the entrance or gate; and several castles are situated on small eminences and on the side of the bay. But the town is commanded by the hill of Popo. Though possessing an excellent harbour, C. presents to a stranger's eye much of the melancholy aspect of a cloister. Long galleries,-short and clumsy columns, - streets narrow and dark, from the too great projection of the terraces, which almost prevent the admission of day-light, - the greater part of the houses dirty, full of smoke, poverty-stricken, and sheltering beings still more filthy, black, and miserable, -such is the picture at first presented by a city adorned with the name of the rival of Rome. However, on entering the houses, their construction, singular at first sight, appears to be well-contrived, the grand object being to admit the circulation of fresh air. The rooms are nothing but immense vestibules, in which the cool air, unfortunately so rare, might be respired with the utmost delight, were it not for the stings of thousands of insects, and for the bats, whose bites are not only more painful but are even said to be venomous. A table, half-a-dozen wooden chairs, a mat-bed, a large jar, and two candlesticks, generally compose the whole stock of furniture of these habitations, which are built of brick, and covered in with tiles. The city is strong, and of vast extent; but 9,000 men at least would be required to defend it at all points. The immense cisterns contained within its walls, are justly objects of admiration; and the water preserved in them is excellent; but C. is rather a fortified than a commercial town, and will entirely cease to be the latter when it is no longer the entrepot of Panama. The cathedral church is magnificent.—C., on account of its advantageous situation, and the excellence and safety of its harbour, very soon became the most important settlement and emporium of the Spaniards in South America: so early as 1544, it was a town of some commercial celebrity. Being selected as the port in which the galleons should first trade on their arrival from Europe, and to which they were ordered to repair previous to their voyage homeward, the resort to it was so great, and its sources of prosperity so copious, that in a short time it rose to be one of the most populous and opulent cities in America. And though it has declined very much, it still carries on a considerable inland and foreign trade, and preserves no small portion of its comparative importance. It is reckoned by Humboldt before all the other ports, with the exception of Vera Cruz, the Havannah, and Lima. Including the small adjoining ports of Rio-Hacha, Santa-Maria, and Portobello, with which it is connected by intimate commercial relations, it exported annually, towards the close of last cent., of the produce of native agriculture to the value of £252,000 sterling. The quantities of that produce were as follows: cotton, 3,307,500 lbs.; sugar, 220,500 lbs.; indigo, 22,050 lbs.; Brazil wood, 882,000 lbs.; quinquina, of ipecacuanha, 13,230 lbs. Besides these articles there was a considerable exportation of the precious metals to Old Spain. The value of the importation amounted to £840,000 sterling annually. The commerce of C. has greatly declined of late years. The imports are almost exclusively for the consumption of the province itself, and the export trade is reduced aled by the steamers of the Royal mail steam-packet 1 company to England. Produce is exported chiefly from Savanilla, a port 60 m. to windward of C., and from the small port of Zapote close to C. But the opening of the dique or canal connecting the bay of C. with the Magdalena, will, it is expected, enable C. to resume its place among the ports of export. This canal will be about 80 m. in length, extending from the Magdalena near Barranca, to Pasa-Cavallos in the bay of C. The quantity of goods imported in British bottoms in 1843 was £64,551; in New Granadian, £52,189. In 1845, the amount imported in British bottoms had fallen to £25,321; that in New Granadian to £19,236. In 1846, 42 vessels <math>= 6,502</math> tons, entered at the port of C. Of these <math>12 = 2,621</math> tons, were British. The total value of their cargoes was £62,708; of which £23,921 was British. The value of the exports in the same year was £48,712.—The bay of C., on which depends so much of its commercial consequence, extends 21 leagues from N to S. It has a sufficient depth of water, and good anchorage, and is well-sheltered. There are many shallows, however, at the entrance; and on some of these there is so very little water that a skilful pilot is always requisite to steer the vessels into it with safety. The difficulty of navigating vessels in it is also increased by the irregularity of the tides, which increase the danger of striking, and by the slimy and tenacious consistence of the bottom, which, when a vessel does run aground, often renders it necessary to lighten her before she can be got off. C. is 280 m. sailing distance from Chagres; 470 m. from Kingston in Jamaica; and 180 m. from Santa-Martha. The importance and wealth of the place have made it the object of frequent attacks. In 1544, it was taken and pillaged by some French adventurers, under the direction of a Corsican pilot. About forty years afterwards, half of it was laid in ashes by Sir Francis Drake, and the rest was saved from destruction by a ransom of 120,000 silver ducats. It was a third time invaded, in 1597, by the French, under the command of M. de Pointis, to whom it capitulated. It was again besieged in 1740, by the English under Vernon, but it held out successfully, and the besiegers were obliged to retire after a great loss of men and labour. The pop. of C. amounts to about 12,000, and is for the most part composed of people of colour, the greater portion of whom are sailors or fishermen. Their petulance and vivacity form a singular contrast with the indifference and mildness of those who are called Whites; so that, notwithstanding their idleness, they appear active and laborious. The contraband trade is exclusively confined to them; and the heartiness with which they engage in it is a reproach to those whose duty it is to put a stop to the illicit traffic. The women of colour—the offspring of negresses and white men-are tall, and much more agreeable than the mulattoes of our Antilles. The place is unhealthy, and within the last year has been dreadfully depopulated by cholera. The therm. generally ranges from 80° to 86° in the shade.
CARTHKENNY, a river of S Wales, which runs into the Tave, 7 m. WSW of Carmarthen.
CARTIER'S CREEK, a river of Pennsylvania which runs into the Ohio, in lat. 42° 2' N.
CARTIGNIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. and 5 m. SW of Avesnes, on the Helpe. Pop. 1,701.

CARTINAAD, or CADUTINADA, a small district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, between the chain of the E Ghauts and the Indian ocean. It is generally fertile and well cultivated; and produces rice and pepper in large quantities, and in some parts car- CAR is governed by a rajah who resides at Cootaport, and who pays his tribute in pepper.
CARTMEL, a town and parish in the co. of Lancaster, on a promontory which runs out into the Irish channel, 10 m. from Ulverstone, and 16 m. from Lancaster. Area of p. 23,960 acres. Pop. 4,927.
CARTMEL-FELL, a chapelry in the above p. of Cartmel. Area 2,900 acres. Pop. 356.
CARTOCETO, a village in the States-of-the- Church, deleg. of Urbin-et-Pesaro, 12 m. SE of Urbin.
CARTWORTH, a township in the p. of Kirk- Burton, 6 m. S of Huddersfield. Area 2,820 acres. Pop. 382.
CARTWRIGHT, a township in the Newcastle district of Canada West, on the SE side of Lake Scugog. Pop. 445.
CARUALLEDA, or CARVALLEDA, a small town of Venezeula, 240 m. E of Coro.
CARUPANO, a coast town of Venezeula, in the prov. and 60 m. E by N of Cumana, and 2 m. E of Salinas point, at the opening of two fine valleys. Pop. 800. It conducts a trade in horses and mules.

CARUR, or KARUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 75 m. E of Coimbatoor, on the N side of the Amaravati, 6 m. W of its junction with the Caveri, in N lat. 10° 58', and E long. 78° 8'. It consists of about 1,000 houses, and has a strong fort and a large Hindu temple. The vicinity is productive, especially in grain and sugar-cane. The advantages of its situation, and the luxuriance of the surrounding district, whose surface, with the exception of a few isolated hills, is a dead flat, rendered it formerly an important commercial emporium.
CARVAJALES. See CARBAJALES.
CARVALHO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 18 m. SE of Coimbra, at the foot of a hill of the same name rising to an alt. of 679 ft.
CARVEL-DE-SAN-THOMAS, or the BERGANTIN, an island, or rather high rock in the West Indies, between the Virgin isles on the E, and Porto Rico on the W.
CARVER, a township in Plymouth co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S. Iron-ore is procured in the vicinity. Pop. 995.
CARVIEYRO, or CARBUEYRO (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of Algarve in Portugal, forming the extremity of the Serra-de-Monchique in N lat. 39° 21′ 48″.

CARVIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Bethune .- The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 17,200. -The com., 16 m. E of Bethune, had a pop. in 1841 of 5,053.

CARWAR, a sea-port town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, situated on a bay at the mouth of a navigable river, 55 m. S by E of Goa. This was formerly a place of considerable trade, and exported cloth, but has fallen to decay. An English factory was established here about 1640, which traded with Persia and Arabia. C. was ceded to Britain in 1799.
CARYOCA, or more properly Caryboca, a fine cascade in the Carcovado hills, in the vicinity of Riode-Janeiro, near the spot where the valley declines from Laranjeiras towards the suburb of Catete.
CARY'S ISLANDS, a group of small islands in the N part of Baffin's bay, in N lat. 77°, W long. 73°.

CARYSFORD REEF, a line of rocks in the gulf of Florida, near the coast of East Florida. The lightvessel moored near its W edge is in N lat. 25° 12', W long. 80° 16′ 30″, 32 m. from Florida light-house.
CARYSFORT, a village in the p. of Rathdrum, co. Wicklow, 41 m. SW of Rathdrum. It was formerly a borough.
CARYSFORT (CAPE), a promontory on the coast damons. The W portion is covered with wood. It of East Falkland island, 2 m. N of Cow bay.
CARYSTO (CAPE), a promontory on the S coast of the island of Negropont, in 37° 55', E long. 24° 32'. -A small island of the same name lies off it.
CASA-BLANCA. See BLANCA.
CASA (RIO DE), a river on the N coast of the isle of Trinidad, falling into the sea 4 leagues E of the NW extremity of the island, and to the W of the Ojos-del-Agua mountain in the Sierra-de-Casas.
CASAC. See KAZAK.
CASA-BRANCA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of San-Paulo. Pop. of district 3,000.—Also a village in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, on the road from Queluz to Sabara, 12 m. ENE of Marianna.
CASACALENDA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, 8 m. SSW of Larino. Pop. 5,190. Wine, fruit, and silk, are produced in the environs.

CASACCIA, or Kasatsch, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the Grisons, 30 m. SSE of Coirè, near the source of the Maira, and at the foot of the Septimer and the Maloïa. Pop. 152. It forms a commercial entrepot between Coirè, Chiavenna, and the Tyrol.
CASACANCHA, a small village of Peru, on the road from Lima to Pasco, between Culluai and Pulcomayo.
